title: Centre of mass of continuous mass distribution

concepts.title: Centre of mass for a rod
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of mass for a Rod

(a) Uniform rod of length L

${{\text{x}}_{\text{cm}}}=\frac{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{L}}\text{x}\frac{\text{M}}{\text{L}}\text{dx}}{\text{M}}=\left. \frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ L}}\frac{{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{2} \right|_{\text{x}=0}^{\text{x}=1}=\frac{\text{L}}{2}$

(b) Non-uniform rod of length $\text{L}$, whose linear density is given by : 

$\lambda ={{\lambda }_{0}}\left( 1+\frac{\text{x}}{\text{L}} \right)$

Solution : 

Assume an element of thickness $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the axis,

then, $d\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}=\left( \lambda \text{dx} \right)={{\lambda }_{0}}\left( 1+\frac{\text{x}}{\text{L}} \right)\text{dx}$

Thus, the centre of mass is given by

$\therefore \quad \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{\int \mathrm{xdm}}{\int \mathrm{dm}}$

$=\frac{\int_0^{\mathrm{L}} \mathrm{x} \lambda_0\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{L}}\right) \mathrm{dx}}{\int_0^{\mathrm{L}} \lambda_0\left(1+\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{L}}\right) \mathrm{dx}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{L}^2}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{L}^3}{3 \mathrm{~L}}}{\mathrm{~L}+\frac{\mathrm{L}^2}{2 \mathrm{~L}}}=\frac{\left(5 \mathrm{~L}^2\right) 2}{(3 \mathrm{~L}) 6}$

$=\left(\frac{5 \mathrm{~L}}{9}\right)$

concepts.title: Centre of mass for a circular arc
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of mass for a circular arc

Assume an element of thickness $\text{Rd}\theta $ over the arc $\text{AB}$.

then

Assume an element of thickness $\mathrm{dx}$ at a distance $\mathrm{x}$ from the axis, then,

$\mathrm{dm}=\lambda \operatorname{Rd} \theta$

centre of mass will lie along the axis of symmetry OP. Hence,

and $\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{\int \mathrm{ydm}}{\int \mathrm{dm}}=\frac{\frac{\int_{\theta_0}^{\frac{\theta_0}{2}}(\mathrm{R} \cos \theta) \lambda \mathrm{R} d \theta}{\int_{\frac{-\theta_0}{2}}^2} \lambda \mathrm{Rd} \theta}{\frac{\theta_0}{2}}$

$=\frac{2 \mathrm{R}\left(\sin \frac{\theta_0}{2}\right)}{\theta_0}$

 

Tip : Try to identify the axis of symmetry, wherever possible in case of calculating COM

Result : 

(1) Centre of mass of an arc subtending an angle $\theta_0$ at centre $=2 R\left(\frac{\sin \theta_0 / 2}{\theta_0}\right)$ [from centre on axis of symmetry

(2) COM of semi-circular ring = $\frac{2 \mathrm{R}}{\pi}$ $\left\{\text { Put here } \theta_0=\pi\right\}$

(3) $\text{COM}$ of a quarter circle arc $=\frac{2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{R}}{\pi}$ $\left\{\text { Put } \theta_0=\frac{\pi}{2}\right\}$

concepts.title: Centre of mass for a lamina subtending angle theta
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of mass for a Uniform Laminar arc 

Consider an arc at a radius $r$ from the centre $\text{O}$

$\text{dm}=\sigma \left( 2\pi \text{r} \right)\text{dr}\times \frac{{{\theta }_{0}}}{2\pi }$

and ${{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}=\frac{\int \text{Ydm}}{\int \text{dm}}$

$=\frac{\int _{{{R}_{1}}}^{{{R}_{2}}}\left( \frac{2r\text{sin}{{\theta }_{0}}/2}{{{\theta }_{0}}} \right)\sigma \left( {{\theta }_{0}}r \right)dr}{\int _{{{R}_{1}}}^{{{R}_{2}}}\sigma \left( {{\theta }_{0}}r \right)dr}$

${{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}=\left( \frac{4\text{sin}\left( \frac{{{\theta }_{0}}}{2} \right)}{3{{\theta }_{0}}} \right)\frac{\left( \text{R}_{2}^{3}-\text{R}_{1}^{3} \right)}{\left( \text{R}_{2}^{2}-\text{R}_{1}^{2} \right)}$

${{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}=\frac{4\text{R}}{3{{\theta }_{0}}}\text{sin}\left( \frac{{{\theta }_{0}}}{2} \right)$ {When

Remember : 

  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ CM}$ of an arc-shaped disc $=\frac{4\text{R}}{3{{\theta }_{0}}}\left( \text{sin}{{\theta }_{0}}/2 \right)$
  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ CM}$ of a semi-circular disc $=\left( \frac{4\text{R}}{3\pi } \right)$
  • $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ CM of a quarter disc $=\frac{4\text{R}}{3\pi }\left( 2 \right)\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\left( \frac{4\sqrt{2}\text{R}}{3\pi } \right)$

concepts.title: Centre of mass of hemispherical shell
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of mass of Hemi-spherical shell

Consider a ring at an angle $\theta $ from vertical axis of symmetry

$\text{dm}=\left[ \left( 2\pi \text{Rsin}\theta \right)\text{Rd}\theta \right]$

$\therefore$ $\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{CM}} \frac{\int_0^{\pi / 2} \mathrm{Ydm}}{\int_0^{\pi / 2} \mathrm{dm}}$

$=\frac{\int_0^{\pi / 2}\left(2 \pi \mathrm{R}^2 \sin \theta\right)(\sigma \mathrm{d} \theta) \mathrm{R} \cos \theta}{\int_0^{\pi / 2} 2 \pi \sigma \mathrm{R}^2 \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta}$

$=\frac{\mathrm{R} \int_0^{\pi / 2} \sin \theta \cos \theta \mathrm{d} \theta}{\int_0^{\pi / 2} \sin \theta \mathrm{d} \theta}=\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}$

Remember : 

COM of hemi-spherical shell lies on $\frac{R}{2}$ distance from centre on axis of symmetry.

When surface mass density varies as $\sigma=\sigma_0 \cos \theta$ 

${{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}=\frac{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\left( \text{Rcos}\theta \right)\left[ {{\sigma }_{0}}\text{cos}\theta \left( 2\pi \text{Rsin}\theta \right)\text{Rd}\theta \right]}{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\left[ {{\sigma }_{0}}\text{cos}\theta \left( 2\pi \text{Rsin}\theta \right)\text{Rd}\theta \right]}=\frac{\text{R}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{2}}\theta \text{sin}\theta \text{d}\theta }{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\pi /2}\text{sin}\theta \text{cos}\theta \text{d}\theta }=\frac{2\text{R}}{3}$

concepts.title: Centre of mass of a solid hemisphere
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of mass of a Solid Hemisphere

Consider thin concentric shell at a distance $r$ of thickness $\text{dr}$ let volume mass density $={{\rho }_{0}}$ $\text{dm}={{\rho }_{0}}\left( 2\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)\text{dr}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}=\frac{\int ~\text{Ydm}}{\int ~\text{dm}}=\frac{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{R}}\left( \frac{\text{r}}{2} \right)\rho \left( 2\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)\text{dr}}{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{R}}\rho \left( 2\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}} \right)\text{dr}}$

$\left\{ {} \right.$ Here taking $\text{Y}=\frac{\text{r}}{2}$ as calculated in case of hemi-spherical shell $\}$

$=\frac{{{\text{R}}^{4}}\times 3}{2\times 4\times {{\text{R}}^{3}}}=\left( \frac{3\text{R}}{8} \right)$

Remember : 

Centre of mass of a hemi-spherical disc of radius $\text{R}$ lies on $\left( \frac{3\text{R}}{8} \right)$ distance from centre on axis of symmetry.

concepts.title: Centre of mass of a hollow right cone
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of mass of a hollow right cone 

Consider a ring of thickness dx having a slant height $\text{x}$ and vertical height $\text{h}$.

Now $\text{dm}=\sigma \left( 2\pi \text{xsin}\theta \right)\text{dx}$

${{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}=\frac{\int ~\text{Ydm}}{\int ~\text{dm}}=\frac{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{~~~\frac{\text{H}}{\text{cos}\theta }}\text{h}\sigma \left( 2\pi \text{xsin}\theta \right)\text{dx}}{\frac{\text{H}}{\text{cos}\theta }\sigma \left( 2\pi \text{xsin}\theta \right)\text{dx}}$

also, $\text{ h}=\text{xcos}\theta$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} = & \frac{\text{cos}\theta \mathop{\int }_{0}^{~~~\frac{H}{\text{cos}\theta }}{{x}^{2}}dx}{\mathop{\int }_{0}^{~~~\frac{H}{\text{cos}\theta }}xdx}=\frac{2}{3}\frac{H\text{cos}\theta }{\text{cos}\theta } \\ {{Y}_{CM}} & ~=\frac{2}{3}H\text{ from }~\text{ the }~\text{ point }~\text{ }O \\ \end{array}$

concepts.title: Centre of mass of a solid right cone
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of mass of Solid Right Cone 

$\text{dm}={{\rho }_{0}}\left( \pi {{r}^{2}} \right)dx$

where $r=x\text{tan}\theta $

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ dm}={{\rho }_{0}}\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta \left( \pi {{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)\text{dx}$

${{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}=\frac{\int \text{Ydm}}{\int \text{dm}}=\frac{\int _{0}^{\text{H}}\text{x}{{\rho }_{0}}\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta \left( \pi {{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)d\text{x}}{\int _{0}^{\text{H}}{{\rho }_{0}}\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta \left( \pi {{\text{x}}^{2}} \right)\text{dx}}$

$=\frac{3{{\text{H}}^{4}}}{4{{\text{H}}^{3}}}=\frac{3\text{H}}{4}$

Remember : 

$\text{CM}$ of a hollow right circular cone $=\frac{2\text{H}}{3}$

$\text{CM}$ of a solid right circular cone $=\left( \frac{3\text{H}}{4} \right)$

concepts.title: Centre of mass of a body with portion removed
concepts.conceptBody:
Centre of mass of a body with portion removed

Example :

From a uniform circular disc of radius R, a smaller disc of radius R/2, with circumference touching each other is scooped out. Find out the CM of remaining body. 

Solution : 

Steps of solving 

  1. Fill the cavity by mass $+\text{m},-\text{m}$
  2. Now, centre of mass can be calculated.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ co-ordinates of $\text{CM}=\left( -\text{R}/6,0 \right)$

$\because {{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}$ will remain same at origin the remaining portion is symmetric about $\text{x}$-axis)

Example :

From a uniform solid hemisphere of radius R, a solid right circular cone of radius R/2 and height R/2 is scooped out (a) symmetrically (b) unsymmetrically. Find the position of centre of mass in each case .

Solution : 

(a) Symmetrical cavity 

Fill the cavity with $+\text{m}$ and $-\text{m}$ mass.

let $\text{M}$ be the masses of the original hemisphere (when the cone is not scooped out) then

$\text{M}=\frac{2}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{3}}\times {{\rho }_{0}}$

now, let $\text{m}$ be the mass of cone scooped out, then

$\text{m}=\frac{1}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}{{\rho }_{0}}=\frac{1}{3}\pi {{\left( \frac{\text{R}}{2} \right)}^{3}}{{\rho }_{0}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{M}}{\text{m}}=\frac{\frac{2}{3}\pi {{\text{R}}^{3}}}{\frac{1}{3}\pi {{\left( \frac{\text{R}}{2} \right)}^{3}}}=16$

Now, ${{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}=\frac{\text{M}{{\text{y}}_{1}}-\text{m}{{\text{y}}_{2}}}{\text{M}-\text{m}}$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {{\text{y}}_{1}}=\frac{3\text{R}}{8},{{\text{y}}_{2}}=\frac{\text{H}}{4}=\frac{\text{R}}{8} \\ {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{Y}}_{\text{CM}}}=\frac{\left( \frac{\text{M}}{\text{m}} \right){{\text{y}}_{1}}-{{\text{y}}_{2}}}{\left( \frac{\text{M}}{\text{m}} \right)-1}=\frac{\left( 16 \right)\left( \frac{3\text{R}}{8} \right)-\frac{\text{R}}{8}}{16-1} \\ {} & ~=\frac{47\text{R}}{120} \\ \end{array}$

(b) Unsymmetrical cavity 

Her, also $\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{m}}=16$ 

$\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{m}}\right) \mathrm{X}_1-\mathrm{X}_2}{\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{m}}-1}=\frac{16 \times 0-\mathrm{R} / 2}{16-1}=\frac{-\mathrm{R}}{30}$

Now, $\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{CM}}=\frac{\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{m}}\right) \mathrm{y}_1-\mathrm{y}_2}{\left(\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{m}}\right)-1}=\frac{47 \mathrm{R}}{120}$

$\therefore \quad$ Co-ordinates of COM are $\left(\frac{-\mathrm{R}}{30}, \frac{47 \mathrm{R}}{120}\right)$

examples.title: The $\mathrm{Y}$ axis of centre of gravity of semicircular plate $1.32 \mathrm{~m}$ diameter from its base as shown in figure.

If three object $A, B$ and $C$ of mass $m, 2 m$ and $3 m$ are placed along the $x$-axis at distance $3 x, 2 x$ and $x$ from its origin. In this case, the distance of the centre of mass from the origin is

An object as shown in the figure consists of a sphere of mass $1 \mathrm{~kg}$ and radius $5 \mathrm{~cm}$ attached to a rod of length $50 \mathrm{~cm}$ and mass $500 \mathrm{~g}$. If this has to be balanced horizontally on a knife-edge, at what distance from point A must the knife-edge be placed?

As shown in fig. when a spherical cavity (centred at $O$ ) of radius 1 is cut out of a uniform sphere of radius $R$ (centred at $C$ ), the centre of mass of remaining (shaded) part of sphere is at $G$, i.e on the surface of the cavity. $R$ can be determined by the equation :
